title: Ensure a support access group has been created to manage  incidents with IBM Support

input_row.title: Ensure a support access group has been created to manage  incidents with IBM Support
input_row.description: If you experience problems with IBM Cloud®, you can use support cases to get help with technical, access (IAM), billing & usage, and invoice or sales inquiry issues. You can create and manage a support case by using the Support Center. Access to Support Center is governed via IAM roles. In order to access Support Center, a user must have viewer or higher on the Support Center service. To create or edit service cases, a user must have editor or higher on the Support Center service. After you submit a support case, the support team investigates the issue based on your type of support plan. The types of available support depends on the support level of the account. Your support plan also determines the severity level that you can assign to support cases. For more information, see Case severity and response time.
input_row.rationale: Support cases are used to raise issues to IBM Cloud. Access to IBM Cloud Support Center is managed via IAM roles. IAM roles can be used to efficiently control which users in an organization can view support cases, which can view, edit, and delete support cases, and which have no access at all. You can configure different levels of Support Center access using Access Groups.
input_row.audit: 1. In the IBM Cloud console, go to Manage > Access (IAM), and then select Access Groups. 2. Look for an access group relating to Support Center. Access Group names are customizable and vary from customer to customer. 3. To verify the access policies for an Access Group, click on an Access Group name and then click Access policies. 4. Check for access policies on the Support Center service.
input_row.remediation: 1. In the IBM Cloud console, go to Manage > Access (IAM), and then select Access Groups. 2. Select Create Access Group. 3. Give the Access Group a descriptive name, for example, Support Center Viewers or Support Center Admins. 4. Optionally, provide a brief description. 5. Click Create. 6. Once the Access Group is created, click on the Access Policies tab. 7. Click Assign Access. 8. Click on Account Management and select Support Center from the drop down menu. 9. Select the Support Center role(s) that meet your use case. Descriptions are provided for each role in the IAM UI. 10. Click add. 11. Click Assign. 12. Click on the Users tab. 13. Click Add users 14. Select users from the list and click Add to group.
input_row.impact: Users with access to IBM Cloud Support Center can create and/or manage support tickets based on their IAM role. Support Center access should be managed and assigned using Access Groups.
input_row.default_value: By default, users do not have access to IBM Cloud Support Center.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_iam_access_group_support_center_access_configured

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. Use IBM Cloud CLI command `ibm cloud iam access-groups` to list all access groups.
2. Check for the existence of any access group that is related to the Support Center (by naming convention like "Support Center Viewers" or "Support Center Admins").
3. For each access group found, use the command `ibm cloud iam access-group show <access_group_id>` to fetch details regarding the access policies.
4. Validate that appropriate roles (Viewer or Editor) are assigned to the Support Center service by looking for the `service` field with a value of `support` in the access policies under each access group.
5. Pass criteria: At least one access group must exist with proper roles assigned (viewer/editor) for the Support Center. If none exist or roles are not sufficient, it will be a fail condition.
6. Report the access group name(s), policies attached, and compliance status (Pass/Fail) based on the presence of the appropriate assignments.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The task involves checking the configuration of access groups and their roles, which can be effectively automated using IBM Cloud CLI commands. The use of IBM Cloud CLI to list access groups and check their policies aligns with the capabilities provided by IBM Cloud APIs and CLI tools.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The IBM Cloud CLI command `ibm cloud iam access-groups` is valid for listing access groups, and `ibm cloud iam access-group show <access_group_id>` is appropriate for fetching details of specific access groups.
- However, the program name `ibm_iam_access_group_support_center_access_configured` could be more descriptive. It does not clearly specify the security check being performed, such as role verification for the Support Center.
- The steps outlined are generally complete, but the description of how to identify the correct access group by naming convention could be more explicit.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The program name could be improved for clarity. A suggestion is `ibm_iam_access_group_support_center_role_check` to better reflect the specific check being performed.
- The assessment could include a step to verify that the access group names follow a specific naming convention or pattern, which would make the automation more robust.
- Consider checking for any recent changes or updates in IBM Cloud CLI or API documentation to ensure the commands used are up-to-date and optimal.
- It might be beneficial to include a step that verifies the IAM roles directly against the Support Center service to ensure there are no discrepancies.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-structured and uses appropriate IBM Cloud CLI commands to automate the compliance check. The technical details are mostly accurate, and the approach is feasible given the capabilities of IBM Cloud CLI.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Update the program name to `ibm_iam_access_group_support_center_role_check` for better clarity and descriptiveness. Additionally, consider adding a verification step for the naming convention of access groups to enhance the robustness of the automation.
